filings.mda: Net income for the third quarter of 2024 was $12.9 billion, or $4.37 per diluted share, compared with net income of $13.2 billion, or $4.33 per diluted share, for the prior year quarter. Net revenue was $43.3 billion, up 7% year-over-year. Net interest income (NII) was $23.5 billion, an increase of 3% from the prior year, driven by higher rates on interest-earning assets partially offset by higher funding costs. The Firm raised its full-year 2024 NII guidance to approximately $92.5 billion, excluding Markets. Noninterest revenue increased 14% to $19.8 billion, primarily reflecting higher asset management fees and investment banking revenue. Investment banking fees of $2.3 billion rose 31% YoY, marking the third consecutive quarter of recovery. The Consumer & Community Banking segment reported net income of $4.2 billion on revenue of $17.8 billion. Average deposits increased 2% to $1.28 trillion. Credit card sales volume grew 6% to $264 billion and outstanding balances rose 11% to $232 billion. We continue to observe net charge-off normalization in our Card Services portfolio, with NCO rates of 3.53%, up from 2.93% in Q3 2023, remaining within our previously guided range. Commercial Banking delivered net income of $1.4 billion, with middle market loan demand showing early signs of stabilization. Asset & Wealth Management AUM grew 23% to $3.9 trillion, driven by market appreciation and net inflows of $55 billion. Our Markets business generated $8.0 billion in revenue, up 8%, with Fixed Income Markets up 2% and Equity Markets up 27%. We remain focused on disciplined expense management while continuing to invest in technology and talent. Adjusted overhead ratio was 55%, within our target range.
filings.risk_disclosures: Credit Risk: Net charge-offs for consumer loans increased to $2.2 billion in Q3 2024, up 24% year-over-year, reflecting ongoing normalization from historically low levels. We maintain robust allowance for credit losses of $24.3 billion, representing 1.87% of total loans. We are closely monitoring commercial real estate (CRE) office exposures totaling $11.2 billion, of which 28% are classified as criticized. Net charge-offs in CRE office increased to $258 million in the quarter. Market Risk: Value-at-Risk (VaR) averaged $108 million in Q3 2024 compared to $91 million in Q3 2023, reflecting elevated geopolitical uncertainty and rate volatility. Our stress VaR remained within board-approved limits. Interest rate sensitivity: a 100bps parallel shift in yield curves would impact NII by approximately +$1.8 billion over 12 months. Liquidity Risk: The Firm's average LCR was 115%, above the regulatory minimum of 100%. Total unencumbered HQLA was $1.2 trillion. We issued $42 billion of long-term debt in Q3 2024, maintaining robust access to wholesale funding markets. Operational Risk: We continue to invest heavily in cybersecurity infrastructure, having committed $2.7 billion to technology risk management in 2024. Regulatory capital remains strong: CET1 ratio of 15.3%, well above the 12.3% regulatory minimum including buffers. Regulatory Risk: The Basel III endgame proposal, if enacted as proposed, would increase our standardized RWAs by approximately $650 billion. We are actively engaging with regulators and anticipate meaningful revisions to the final rule. We are monitoring the evolving landscape of AI regulation and ensuring compliance with emerging frameworks.

filings.mda: Bank of America reported third quarter 2024 net income of $6.9 billion, or $0.81 per diluted share, compared to $7.8 billion, or $0.90 per diluted share, in the year-ago period. Total revenue, net of interest expense, decreased 1% to $25.3 billion. Net interest income declined 3% to $13.9 billion as higher deposit costs and lower loan balances outpaced the benefit of asset repricing. We continue to guide toward NII trough in Q3 2024 and expect sequential improvement beginning Q4 2024 and into 2025, supported by the lower rate environment. Noninterest income increased 2% to $11.4 billion, with Global Markets revenue up 12% and asset management fees rising 15% to $3.3 billion, reflecting strong net client flows of $18 billion. Consumer Banking reported net income of $2.5 billion, with consumer checking accounts growing to 36.9 million. Digital banking users reached 58 million, with 90% of deposit transactions now executed digitally. Average consumer deposits decreased 3% to $944 billion as consumers continued to rotate into higher-yielding alternatives. Global Wealth and Investment Management AUM grew 17% to $4.2 trillion, benefiting from equity market appreciation. Global Banking reported $2.1 billion in net income, with investment banking fees of $1.4 billion, up 18% as M&A activity accelerated. Global Markets produced net income of $1.6 billion on revenue of $4.9 billion. We achieved positive operating leverage for the second consecutive quarter, with expenses declining 2% to $16.5 billion through our Operational Excellence program targeting $1.5 billion in run-rate saves by year-end 2025. Return on average tangible common shareholders' equity was 11.2%.
filings.risk_disclosures: Credit Risk: Net charge-offs increased to $1.5 billion in Q3 2024, representing an annualized NCO rate of 0.54%, up from 0.37% in Q3 2023, reflecting normalization to pre-pandemic levels in credit cards and consumer loans. Credit card NCO rate was 3.7%, approaching our through-the-cycle expectation of 3.5-4.0%. Allowance for credit losses totaled $14.1 billion. CRE office exposure stood at $8.4 billion with 31% classified as criticized, and we recorded $312 million in CRE NCOs in the quarter. We are proactively working with borrowers on maturity extensions and restructurings. Market Risk: Average trading VaR of $67 million was relatively stable quarter-over-quarter. Our securities portfolio had net unrealized losses of $86.4 billion (AOCI), which we expect to continue amortizing as securities mature and rates decline. This represents our most significant balance sheet risk; we have restructured $60 billion of AFS securities since Q4 2023 to reduce this sensitivity. Interest rate exposure: an instantaneous 100bps rate decrease would reduce NII by approximately $2.1 billion in the first year. Liquidity Risk: Average LCR was 118% with $897 billion in global excess liquidity sources. We have $1.0 trillion in available liquidity resources. Operational Risk: Ongoing investments in fraud prevention technology reduced consumer fraud losses 12% year-over-year. We are enhancing our model risk governance framework to address increasing use of AI/ML in credit decisions. Regulatory: CET1 ratio of 11.9%, above our 10.0% internal minimum. We await final Basel III endgame rules and have built capital buffers accordingly.

filings.mda: Citigroup reported net income of $3.2 billion, or $1.51 per diluted share, for the third quarter of 2024, compared to $3.5 billion in the prior year period. Total revenues decreased 1% to $20.3 billion. Net interest income declined 4% to $13.4 billion, as higher deposit costs in our Services businesses and the impact of the rate environment in our consumer franchise more than offset asset repricing. Our Transformation remains on track: we have simplified our organizational structure from five to six interoperable businesses, achieved $2.5 billion in annual run-rate savings, and remain on target for our medium-term RoTCE target of 11-12%. Services revenue, our core institutional franchise, grew 8% to $5.0 billion, with Treasury and Trade Solutions up 9% driven by cross-border transaction volume growth of 14%. Markets revenue was $4.8 billion, down 5% from an exceptionally strong prior year quarter, with Fixed Income down 6% and Equities up 32%. Banking revenues grew 31% to $1.6 billion, reflecting continued recovery in investment banking with advisory fees up 60%. US Personal Banking revenues declined 2% to $5.0 billion as Branded Cards NII decreased 3% due to higher funding costs. Branded Cards purchase sales grew 4% and average loans increased 6%. We continue to exit our legacy international consumer businesses: completed exit from Taiwan consumer in Q3 2024 and remain on track for additional market exits. Average deposits decreased 4% to $1.29 trillion. Expenses decreased 1% to $13.6 billion. ROTCE was 7.0%, reflecting the drag from transformation investments and elevated credit costs. We are firmly committed to our ROTCE targets and the path to unlocking Citi's embedded value.
filings.risk_disclosures: Credit Risk: Net credit losses of $2.2 billion in Q3 2024, up 28% year-over-year, driven by Branded Cards NCO rate increasing to 5.36% from 3.67%, reflecting portfolio seasoning and macroeconomic normalization. We have deliberately tightened credit standards in new card originations since Q4 2023 and expect losses to peak in Q4 2024. ACL ratio of 2.73% of loans reflects our reserve build. Legacy exposure to Argentina remains a watch item; we have $1.5 billion remaining. Market Risk: Average VaR of $104 million, up 15% YoY, driven by higher rates and FX volatility. Our remaining held-to-maturity portfolio of $126 billion had unrealized losses of $21.3 billion. We have reduced net open interest rate risk through targeted hedges. Liquidity Risk: LCR of 117%, NSFR of 124%, both well above regulatory minimums. We maintain $897 billion in liquidity reserves. The Russia wind-down continues with exposure reduced to $7.4 billion from $9.8 billion at year-end. Operational Risk: Consent order remediation remains our top operational priority. We have invested $10 billion in infrastructure improvements since 2020 and expect to complete data quality remediation by mid-2025. OCC identified additional data management deficiencies in Q2 2024 that are being addressed. Cybersecurity remains a material risk given our global footprint across 90+ countries. Regulatory: CET1 ratio of 13.7%, above our 12.3% target. Basel III endgame impact estimated at $50-75 billion additional RWA.

filings.mda: Wells Fargo reported third quarter 2024 net income of $5.1 billion, or $1.42 per diluted share, compared with $5.8 billion, or $1.48 per diluted share, in Q3 2023. Total revenue decreased 2% to $20.4 billion. Net interest income declined 11% to $11.7 billion, primarily reflecting higher rates paid on interest-bearing deposits and lower loan balances, partially offset by higher yields on floating-rate assets. We maintain our full-year NII guidance of down 8-9%. Noninterest income grew 16% to $8.7 billion, driven by higher investment advisory fees, trading gains, and investment banking fees which rose 37% to $672 million as we continue to build out our fee-based businesses to reduce NII sensitivity. Consumer banking average deposits decreased 5% to $872 billion. We continue to operate under the asset cap imposed by the Federal Reserve in 2018; we remain committed to satisfying the remaining requirements and are working constructively with our regulators. The asset cap limits our loan growth and overall balance sheet expansion, which we estimate reduces annual revenue potential by approximately $1.5-2.0 billion versus an unconstrained scenario. Home Lending revenue declined 20% reflecting continued weakness in mortgage origination volumes. Auto Loan originations decreased 19% as we maintained credit discipline. Commercial Banking reported $1.1 billion in net income with modest loan growth in selected verticals. Wealth and Investment Management saw AUM increase 14% to $2.1 trillion. Efficiency ratio improved to 63%, with expenses declining 3% to $13.1 billion, tracking toward our 2024 target of <63% and medium-term target of 50-52%.
filings.risk_disclosures: Credit Risk: Total net charge-offs were $1.1 billion, representing 0.50% of average loans on an annualized basis, up from 0.41% in Q3 2023. Consumer credit showed the most notable increases with auto NCOs at 1.37% (up from 0.91%) and credit card NCOs at 5.02% (up from 3.51%). Commercial real estate office: we have $8.4 billion in non-owner-occupied office loans, with 25% classified as criticized. We have reserved appropriately with CRE-specific ACL of $1.8 billion. Allowance for credit losses totaled $15.1 billion, representing 1.69% of total loans. Market Risk: Average trading VaR of $48 million. The securities portfolio carries $13.9 billion in AOCI net unrealized losses, expected to decline as rates normalize. Our balance sheet is asset-sensitive; a 100bps instantaneous rate decline would reduce NII by $1.9 billion over 12 months. Liquidity Risk: Average LCR of 127%, our highest among peers, reflecting conservative liquidity management. HQLA of $186 billion. We maintain diverse funding sources across retail deposits, FHLB advances, and wholesale markets. Regulatory/Legal Risk: The asset cap continues to be our most significant regulatory constraint. Multiple regulatory matters and litigation remain outstanding, including $3.7 billion in contingent liabilities. We received a consent order from the OCC related to anti-money laundering processes in Q2 2024, which we are remediating. Regulatory risk remains elevated. Cybersecurity: We have invested $3.1 billion in technology risk and cybersecurity in 2024. CET1 ratio of 11.3% under SCB framework, with our capital return commitment of $20 billion in 2024.

filings.mda: Goldman Sachs reported net revenues of $12.7 billion and net earnings of $2.99 billion, or $8.40 per diluted share, for Q3 2024, compared with net revenues of $11.8 billion and net earnings of $2.06 billion in Q3 2023. Return on equity was 10.4% and ROTE was 11.1%, demonstrating continued progress toward our medium-term ROTE target of 14-16%. Global Banking & Markets delivered its strongest quarter in over two years with revenues of $8.5 billion. Investment Banking revenues of $1.87 billion surged 20% YoY as M&A completions accelerated and the IPO market reopened: we participated in 38 IPOs in Q3, up from 19 in Q3 2023. Backlog is materially above year-ago levels. FICC revenues of $2.96 billion were down 12% from an exceptional Q3 2023, with intermediation revenues up and financing revenues down. Equities revenues of $3.50 billion rose 18%, led by equity derivatives and prime brokerage as hedge fund leverage expanded. Asset & Wealth Management revenues grew 16% to $3.75 billion. Management fees of $2.4 billion rose 11% as AUS reached $3.1 trillion (up 18% YoY) driven by $49 billion in long-term net inflows and market appreciation. We generated $12.8 billion in realized proceeds from alternative investments, ahead of our $60-65 billion full-year target. Our strategic exit from consumer is substantially complete: the Marcus loan portfolio was sold in Q2 2024, GreenSky was sold in Q1 2024, and we have terminated our credit card partnership with General Motors, which will transition away in 2025. Platform Solutions revenues and losses are declining as we wind down this segment. Firmwide expenses of $8.5 billion were up 8%, reflecting higher compensation accruals tied to performance. Headcount declined 3% year-over-year.
filings.risk_disclosures: Market Risk: Average daily VaR of $318 million in Q3 2024, down 4% from Q2 2024 but up 6% YoY, reflecting elevated volatility in credit and interest rate markets. Our largest VaR concentration remains in Interest Rate ($215 million), followed by Credit ($127 million) and Equity ($98 million). Stress VaR for a hypothetical severe market dislocation scenario is $2.8 billion. We have meaningful exposure to private credit markets; our extended duration alternatives portfolio totals $94 billion and is exposed to mark-to-market risk as private credit spreads normalize. Credit Risk: Our Marcus loan portfolio sale substantially eliminates consumer credit risk. Remaining consumer exposure is $3.2 billion. Institutional credit exposures: level 3 assets of $47 billion represent 5.8% of total assets. Our largest credit concentration is in investment-grade corporate bonds ($180 billion). Counterparty credit risk: net exposure post-collateral is $45 billion, down 8% QoQ. Liquidity Risk: Global Core Liquid Assets (GCLA) of $393 billion, representing 52% of total assets — highest in firm history. LCR of 129%. We issued $30 billion of long-term unsecured debt in Q3 2024 and have $8 billion of maturities remaining in 2024. Operational Risk: Our technology risk program focuses on operational resilience and third-party vendor concentration. We have identified cybersecurity as our highest operational risk and invested $1.2 billion in cyber defenses in 2024. The wind-down of Platform Solutions creates operational complexity as we de-integrate systems and transfer customer servicing. Regulatory: CET1 ratio of 14.6%. Proposed Basel III endgame would increase standardized RWAs by approximately $225 billion (41%); we have engaged regulators and expect meaningful revisions. AI governance: we have deployed 300+ AI models in production and established an AI risk committee.
